examples.title: Recursive Descent Parser for S→aSb|ε
examples.description: Implements predictive parsing without backtracking for a simple grammar
examples.code:
FUNCTION parse_S():
 IF current_token == 'a' THEN
 match('a')
 parse_S()
 match('b')
 ELSE IF current_token == '$' OR current_token == 'b' THEN
 // ε-production: do nothing, just return
 RETURN
 ELSE
 ERROR("Syntax error at S")

FUNCTION match(token):
 IF current_token == token THEN
 current_token = get_next_token()
 ELSE
 ERROR("Match failed")

// Main parsing driver
FUNCTION parse():
 current_token = get_first_token()
 parse_S()
 IF current_token == '$' THEN
 PRINT "Parsing successful"
 ELSE
 PRINT "Syntax error"
examples.explanation: Each non-terminal has a function that checks the lookahead token to decide which production to use. The S() function uses 'a' to choose S→aSb production and accepts ε when lookahead is '$' or 'b'.

examples.title: Predictive Parser Driver Algorithm
examples.description: Table-driven LL(1) parsing algorithm using stack
examples.code:
// Predictive parsing driver
FUNCTION predictive_parse(input, parse_table, start_symbol):
 stack = ['$', start_symbol] // Initialize stack
 pointer = 0 // Input pointer
 
 WHILE stack[top] != '$':
 X = stack[top]
 a = input[pointer] // Current input token
 
 IF X == a: // Terminal matches
 POP stack
 pointer = pointer + 1
 ELSE IF X is terminal: // Terminal doesn't match
 ERROR("Syntax error")
 ELSE IF parse_table[X][a] is EMPTY:
 ERROR("Syntax error - no entry")
 ELSE: // Non-terminal: use parse table
 production = parse_table[X][a]
 POP stack
 RHS = production.RHS // Right-hand side
 // Push RHS in reverse order
 FOR each symbol in RHS (reverse order):
 IF symbol != ε:
 PUSH symbol onto stack
 
 // After loop, stack should have '$'
 IF stack[top] == '$' AND a == '$':
 PRINT "Accept"
 ELSE
 PRINT "Error"
examples.explanation: The driver algorithm repeatedly checks the top of stack. If it matches the input, pops and advances. If it's a non-terminal, looks up the parse table and replaces with the production's RHS in reverse order.

examples.title: FIRST Set Computation Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to compute FIRST sets for all grammar symbols
examples.code:
FUNCTION compute_FIRST():
 // Initialize FIRST sets
 FOR each terminal t:
 FIRST(t) = {t}
 FOR each non-terminal N:
 FIRST(N) = {}
 
 REPEAT
 changed = FALSE
 
 FOR each production A → α:
 // Case 1: X is terminal
 IF first_symbol(α) is terminal t:
 IF t not in FIRST(A):
 ADD t to FIRST(A)
 changed = TRUE
 
 // Case 2: X is non-terminal
 IF first_symbol(α) is non-terminal X:
 IF ε in FIRST(X):
 // Add FIRST(X) - {ε} to FIRST(A)
 FOR each symbol in FIRST(X):
 IF symbol != ε AND symbol not in FIRST(A):
 ADD symbol to FIRST(A)
 changed = TRUE
 
 // Check second symbol, third symbol...
 FOR each symbol Yi in α:
 IF ε in FIRST(all previous symbols):
 IF first_symbol(Yi) is terminal t:
 ADD t to FIRST(A)
 ELSE IF first_symbol(Yi) is non-terminal Z:
 ADD FIRST(Z) - {ε} to FIRST(A)
 ELSE:
 // ε not in FIRST(X), just add FIRST(X)
 ADD FIRST(X) - {ε} to FIRST(A)
 
 // Case 3: α can derive ε (all symbols nullable)
 IF ALL symbols in α can derive ε:
 IF ε not in FIRST(A):
 ADD ε to FIRST(A)
 changed = TRUE
 
 UNTIL NOT changed
 
 RETURN FIRST sets
examples.explanation: The algorithm iteratively computes FIRST sets by adding terminals directly and non-terminal sets, handling ε-productions by checking if all preceding symbols can derive ε.

examples.title: LL(1) Parse Table Construction
examples.description: Algorithm to build LL(1) parsing table from FIRST and FOLLOW sets
examples.code:
FUNCTION construct_parse_table(grammar):
 // Initialize parse table with empty entries
 parse_table = empty table
 
 // Compute FIRST and FOLLOW
 FIRST = compute_FIRST(grammar)
 FOLLOW = compute_FOLLOW(grammar)
 
 FOR each production A → α in grammar:
 // For each terminal a in FIRST(α)
 FOR each terminal a in FIRST(α):
 IF a != ε:
 parse_table[A][a] = A → α
 
 // If ε is in FIRST(α), add FOLLOW entries
 IF ε in FIRST(α):
 FOR each terminal b in FOLLOW(A):
 parse_table[A][b] = A → α
 
 // For $ (end of input)
 IF ε in FIRST(α) AND '$' in FOLLOW(A):
 parse_table[A]['$'] = A → α
 
 RETURN parse_table

// Check if grammar is LL(1)
FUNCTION is_LL1(parse_table):
 FOR each cell in parse_table:
 IF cell has MORE THAN ONE entry:
 RETURN FALSE // Conflict exists
 RETURN TRUE
examples.explanation: The parse table is filled by adding production A→α to column 'a' for each 'a' in FIRST(α). If α can derive ε, also add entries for all terminals in FOLLOW(A). Grammar is LL(1) if no cell has multiple entries (conflicts).
